[**Index	22**](#index)

# PURPOSE {#purpose}

This manual guides users and administrators through the use and management of the Dashboards & Analytical Platform implemented for KSEB. It is intended to streamline how data is consumed, explored, and managed across the organization.

## Audience  {#audience}

* **Data Consumers**: Analysts, field officers, and leadership accessing dashboards for insights.

* **Platform Administrators**: Personnel managing data source integration and configuration.

## System Highlights {#system-highlights}

* Low-code data integration and transformation  
* Consistent metadata enforcement  
* Dynamic dashboards for KPIs, trends, and ranked analytics  
* Support for detailed filtering and drill-down analysis

# INTRODUCTON {#introducton}

KSEB is a large public utility with its operational digital footprint spread across several enterprise systems of varying sizes and complexity. Data generated by KSEB is voluminous and highly valuable, but it is distributed across multiple systems, several of them, with very limited integration to reporting interfaces.

This makes reporting difficult, forcing divisional leaders to aggregate data, often using spreadsheets and manual data gathering methods. This distributed data collection results in numbers being at risk of being out of date and often conflicting among different systems.

This platform aims to become a single enterprise data repository for all levels of analytical users across the KSEB organisation, allowing users to instantaneously access subject areas and metrics relevant to them, while also allowing them to inspect the data using standard interactive analysis interfaces.

# CAPABILITIES {#capabilities}

This dashboarding system is built as a low-code platform, allowing KSEB’s data administrators to integrate data sources without requiring developer assistance. The application focuses on the following critical capabilities:

### For Data Consumers (Users) {#for-data-consumers-(users)}

**Navigating the Dashboard**

The dashboard follows a familiar, modular layout:

* **Left Navigation Pane**: Lists subject areas like Operations, Service Delivery, Finance, etc.

* **Main Display Area**: Loads relevant “Insight Cards” upon selection.

### **Insights Cards: Anatomy & Use** {#insights-cards:-anatomy-&-use}

Each card includes:

* Key KPIs: High-level metrics

* Composition Charts: Breakdown of components affecting KPIs

* Trends: Time-based performance plots

* Ranked Analysis: Top/bottom performers across the organization

* Time Selector: Define timeframe of analysis

* Related Analysis Links: Quick jump to related insight sets

### **Filtering & Exploration** {#filtering-&-exploration}

* Use **dynamic filters** to explore performance by division, subdivision, or metric thresholds.

* Drill down into detail via “Related Analysis” or “3-dot menu” on cards.

* Only a subset of data loads by default to ensure system performance.

**Ranked and Comparative Analysis**

* Click the “Expand” icon on any ranked card to:

  * Sort by primary or secondary metric

  * Compare across organizational levels (e.g., districts, zones)

  * Export results to spreadsheet

  * Jump to related analysis subsets

### **Exporting Insights** {#exporting-insights}

* All ranked or filtered datasets can be downloaded in spreadsheet format for reporting.

### System Capabilities {#system-capabilities}

1. Robust **Data Quality Management**: The system enforces string **metadata management**. Metadata elements in the reporting repository must have uniform properties, regardless of where the data is being sourced from. This will avoid duplication and potentially incorrect calculations.  
     
2. The system allows **grouping and hierarchy management** of various metadata elements, allowing data administrators to maintain reporting hierarchies that allow dynamic drill-downs, and create bucketed analyses of various parameters.

![][image1]

3. \`Data administrators have the ability to **add and manage source databases** over standard interfaces.  
4. Administrators may also create **extraction statements** that can be scheduled to extract data systematically from source databases using a highly **flexible internal scheduler**.   
5. Extracted data is loaded into a data table catalogue, which can also be created and managed internally to the application.  
6. Internal metrics and calculations may be maintained by administrators over **dynamic data subsets** for specific analyses.  
7. Specific analysis sets are exposed over a **coded interactive dashboard** that allows users to explore key KPIs, trends, locate anomalies and focus areas.

The following sections will detail building blocks and interaction patterns across the application

# 

# CONSUMING DATA AND INSIGHTS {#consuming-data-and-insights}

This section focuses on data consumers. i.e users that primarily use the platform to derive insights from various subject areas that they have access to.

## ![][image2]

## Interface Structure {#interface-structure}

Application interfaces follow a repeated bento-box style layout to allow users to explore data without requiring repeated training and explanation on each individual data set.

The left navigation panel contains high-level subject areas grouping analyses into broad, high-level categories like Service delivery, operations, financial data etc. When a user selects a category from the left navigation pane, the available analytics cards are made available in the interactive analysis area of the screen.

The user may (based on permissions assigned to their role) be able to switch between subject areas using the left nav or select individual cards to explore deeper into an analysis set, compare values etc.

Upon rendering the page, each card automatically sets context to the latest data available in the system. The user may change this at any time using various exploratory controls available.

## 

## Anatomy of the Insights Card  {#anatomy-of-the-insights-card}

Each insights card follows a standard structure repeated across the platform Selection controls allow users to select between various modes of analysis

1. Key insights:   
   1. KPIs: These are designed to be high-level metrics that need to be constantly monitored by the organisation. These may be represented as numeric values in most cases. There may be one or more key KPI representations available in a single card  
   2. Composition chart: This part usually explains the KPIs, allowing the user to quickly form an understanding of the key components driving/correlated with the KPIs being tracked. The chart may contain interactive navigational triggers that direct the user automatically to related data sets.  
2. Trends: The trend section shows a time series plot that helps the user comprehend how a key metric has performed/changed over time. The analysis window may be adjusted by the user.  
   

![][image3]

3. Ranked Analysis: The ranked analysis tab is a quick way of deriving important insights about how the metric performance is driven by various parts of the organisation. The user may switch between top/bottom performers and have this data displayed at various levels of the organisation. Detail-level analysis may be performed by clicking on the expand icon on the ranked analysis tab.  
4. Timescale selector: This allows selection of the timeframe that is represented within the card. The user may choose a different timescale to switch the data being shown. Once the timescale is set, all elements on the card will automatically be switched to reference the selected timeframe.  
5. Related analysis: This section leads the user to view and explore various datasets published to the analysis group that the card belongs to. Ranked Analysis and detailed analysis sections are detailed in the following sections of this document.

## Ranked Analysis {#ranked-analysis}

![][image4]

Ranked analysis is available for quick access from all analysis cards.   
To perform further detailed analysis, the user may click the “expand” icon below the ranked list on the card.

This interface allows:

1) Exploring a ranked list across various organisational levels, including ranking sections across the state   
2) The ranking is performed using a single metric (see item labeled ranking order on the  image above.)   
3) If the user wishes to further sort based on a secondary metric, this may be added. (see item labelled secondary sort)  
4) User may download the dataset in spreadsheet format  
5) At any time, the user may also switch to another related analysis set (please see item labelled analysis subset selector)

## Exploration \- Analysis Sets {#exploration---analysis-sets}

User may switch to detailed analysis at any time by clicking or the related analysis (3 dots) icon. This takes the user to an interactive analysis secretion, allowing date selections, adding or removing filters etc, to arrive at the exact insight required.

![][image5]

User will also be able to switch between analysis subsets in the group by using the select list on top of the page.

### Interacting With Data {#interacting-with-data}

As this interface is capable of fetching larger volumes of data, to preserve system performance for all users, the platform limits users from directly fetching data for all sections at once.   
Users are required to apply division and subdivision level filters to view data at the section level.

![][image6]

### Dynamic Filtering {#dynamic-filtering}

Once a subset has been selected, this interface also allows users to apply further filtering parameters. For example, only consider sections with more than *n*  complaints in a given month. 

![][image7]

# INTEGRATING & MANAGING DATA (For Data Administrators) {#integrating-&-managing-data-(for-data-administrators)}

![][image8]

The administration section allows data analysts and administrators to integrate data from multiple sources and make it available to users for analysis.  
This module will be made available for users with appropriate privileges as indicated above. If the menu is not visible even though you are in a data administration role, please contact your administrator.

![][image9]

## 

## Setting Up A New Data Source {#setting-up-a-new-data-source}

To set up a new data source, select **Loaders\>Data Sources**  
**![][image10]**

If the source database has already provisioned access, you should be able to supply the credentials here. If the connection is successful, the application will indicate so. 

Once a connection has been successfully set up, you can proceed with writing specific extraction statements against it.

## 

## Creating A New Data Table {#creating-a-new-data-table}

To create a new data table, navigate to **Data\>Data Tables .**

IMPORTANT:  
To create data tables, the dimensional fields to be contained in the data table will need to be already set up in the system.  
Please refer to the section titled “metadata” to understand more.

![][image11]

A column that is a dimensional value will need to be linked to a “structural block” e.g. Section code.

Metrics are numeric values and as such, do not enforce pre-setup from metadata.

Once data tables are set up successfully, the user may proceed with linking an extraction statement to load the data table.

## Setting Up An Extraction Statement {#setting-up-an-extraction-statement}

To set up a new extraction statement against an existing data source, one has to keep the following important prerequisites in mind

IMPORTANT:

1. Make sure that all dates and columns match definitions in the target table. **Otherwise, the table will not load.**  
2. When setting up a load, make sure you have a rough estimate of the data to be scanned and the amount of data to be expected in a single data load.  
3. For the most part, it may be ideal to locate the latest data and only fetch that, as most loader jobs may be set up over a schedule.

Please refer to the sample sql below. (Column and tables may have been intentionally obfuscated in this example).   
**The first part *dates\_const* is used to locate the latest available data in the table.**

| with dates\_const as (  select    row\_number() OVER (      ORDER BY        month\_year desc    ) AS rownum,    month\_year,    rowcount  from    (      select        txn\_month month\_year,        count(1) as rowcount      from        dvw.collection\_data      where        txn\_month \>= to\_char(current\_date \- 70, 'YYYYMM') :: int      group by        txn\_month    ) a1)SELECT  a.txn\_month as month\_year,  a.section\_code,  a.dimension1 as dimension1,  A.dimension2 as dimension2,  /\*list might need updation\*/  case    when a.receipt\_type \= 'OnCounter' then 'Offline'    else 'Online'  end as pmt\_channel\_group,  /\*list channel group ends\*/  sum(metric1) total\_metric\_count,  sum(metric1) total\_metricfrom  d.table\_data a  Inner join dates\_const as dates ON (    a.txn\_month \= dates.month\_year    AND dates.rownum \= 1  )group by  a.txn\_month,  a.section\_code,  a.dimension1,  a.dimension2,  case    when a.receipt\_type \= 'OnCounter' then 'Offline'    else 'Online'  end |
| :---- |

An extraction statement will need to be linked to a loader job, before it can load the target table.

## Loading Data {#loading-data}

![][image12]

### Job Validity  {#job-validity}

A loader job will need to be set up with a Start date and an end date. The job will be submitted to the scheduler only during this broad window and will no longer run as scheduled on expiry of this window.

### Frequency {#frequency}

Cron type indicates the frequency of the job. Daily, weekly, monthly, and yearly frequencies may be available.

![][image13]

Based on the frequency, the job will run at the time specified, on the day specified by the frequency. Day of month, day of week etc may be specified explicitly.

### Dependencies and Load Rules {#dependencies-and-load-rules}

The job needs to be connected to a previously created loader statement to run against a connection available in the system.

The job may be optionally set up to run only if a predecessor job has been completed.  This will ensure that the job does not run even if the  schedule matches, but dependencies have not been met.

## Creating Data Subsets {#creating-data-subsets}

Data tables are not directly used for analysis.  
Data subsets may be created to be “slices” of a data table that best fit the context of analysis, this may even be the subset in its entirety if needed.

![][image14]

Subsets will allow users to apply dynamic filters (like “today”) , or write expressions and aggregation rules. Subsets are “virtual” and will not take up storage in the system.

![][image15] 

Subsets may have internal aggregation and filtering rules, and can therefore be tailored to fit a specific analysis (e.g. “*Arrears outstanding for more than 7 months from HT Consumers that are Government Insititutions*”) 

This will enable this data representation to be consistently used in all analysis sets that the management may be using to formulate operational or strategy execution.

### Subset Groups {#subset-groups}

Once subsets have been created, virtual groups may be created of relevant subsets.   
**IMPORTANT:** Subset groups are mapped to analysis sets made available over dashboards. 

## **Troubleshooting & Support** {#troubleshooting-&-support}

* **Dashboard not updating?** Check if the relevant data job has run.  
* **Missing access?** Confirm your role and privileges with system admin  
* **Data mismatch?** Validate source data and check extraction configurations.  
* **Cannot find a menu item?** Make sure you have the appropriate access. Talk to your data administrator.  
* **Need to Integrate A New Data Source?** Talk to your data administrator. Make sure that the source database has a read only id provisioned with access to the correct source tables.
